skill.name: azure-communication-common-java
skill.description: Guides Java developers through Azure Communication Services common authentication utilities, token credentials, token refresh options, and communication identifiers.
skill.summary: Use Azure Communication Services common authentication and identifier patterns in Java.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: The static command-execution findings are false positives caused by Markdown code fences and inline Java identifiers, not executable Ruby or shell behavior. The hardcoded URLs are Azure service placeholder endpoints or scopes, but one code example prints the first characters of an access token, which can normalize unsafe secret logging.

medium_findings.title: Access Token Prefix Logging in Documentation Example
medium_findings.description: The Token Access example prints the first 20 characters of an access token. Even partial token values can be sensitive in logs, screenshots, and support transcripts, so this pattern should be replaced with metadata-only logging.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited line directly calls token.getToken().substring(0, 20) inside a logging statement. The surrounding text warns not to expose tokens, but the sample still demonstrates partial secret disclosure.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Misclassified as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The external command findings are Markdown code fences or inline Java API names. No Ruby backtick execution, shell command, subprocess invocation, or user-controlled command construction appears at these locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are visible Markdown fences or inline code spans in documentation. They do not execute commands and contain no shell interpreter call.

low_findings.title: Azure Placeholder URLs Misclassified as Suspicious Network Activity
low_findings.description: The URL findings document Azure Communication Services endpoints and an Azure authentication scope. They are placeholders or Microsoft service identifiers, not hidden callbacks, downloaders, or data exfiltration endpoints.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are part of Java and environment-variable examples for Azure Communication Services. No code in the skill performs a network request from the assistant environment.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Finding Has No Supporting Code
low_findings.description: Line 3 is frontmatter describing Azure Communication Services credentials. It contains no weak algorithm name, hashing code, encryption code, or cryptographic configuration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line is a natural-language description only. No cryptographic primitive or implementation choice is present there.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Finding Is Java Type-Checking Documentation
low_findings.description: The system reconnaissance finding points to a Java method that processes communication identifiers. It does not run operating-system discovery commands or collect host information.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line is a Java method declaration inside a documentation example. It has no OS command, process execution, or environment inspection behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: Partial Secret Disclosure Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: The documentation demonstrates logging a token substring. Assistants may reproduce this pattern unless prompted to log only expiry time, token source, or redacted status.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The code explicitly derives text from token.getToken() for output. This is a concrete secret-handling anti-pattern, even though it is not malicious.

content.value_statement: Azure Communication Services clients require careful token and identifier handling. This skill gives Java-focused guidance for credentials, refresh options, cloud environments, and identifier types.
actual_capabilities: Explains how to add the azure-communication-common Java dependency.
Shows static and refreshable CommunicationTokenCredential patterns.
Describes ACS, phone number, Teams, and unknown identifier classes.
Covers Entra ID credential options for Teams Phone Extensibility.
Lists available Azure Communication cloud environments.
Provides common Java patterns for creating Chat clients with refreshed tokens.
limitations: Does not create Azure Communication Services resources or issue tokens.
Does not verify current Azure SDK version compatibility automatically.
Does not include complete application error handling or production retry policies.
Includes documentation examples only; users must adapt them to their own services.

use_cases.title: Add ACS Authentication to a Java Client
use_cases.description: Use the skill to choose static or refreshable token credentials for Chat, Calling, or related ACS clients.

use_cases.title: Normalize Communication Identifiers
use_cases.description: Use the identifier examples to map raw ACS, phone, Teams, and unknown IDs into typed Java objects.

use_cases.title: Review Token Handling Patterns
use_cases.description: Use the best-practice notes to review token refresh, credential disposal, and token exposure risks in Java code.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Credential
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a short-lived Java client
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to explain how I should create an Azure Communication Services Java credential for a short-lived client.

prompt_templates.title: Design Token Refresh
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to design a Java CommunicationTokenCredential setup with proactive token refresh and safe failure handling.

prompt_templates.title: Map Identifier Types
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced identifier parsing and type handling
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to help me parse raw ACS identifiers into the correct Java identifier classes and explain each branch.

prompt_templates.title: Audit ACS Auth Code
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to review my Java ACS authentication code for token exposure, refresh mistakes, and incorrect identifier usage.

output_examples.input: I need a long-lived ACS chat client in Java.
output_examples.output: The assistant explains proactive token refresh, initial token handling, credential construction, and safe cleanup steps.

output_examples.input: How should I represent a Teams user and a phone number?
output_examples.output: The assistant compares MicrosoftTeamsUserIdentifier and PhoneNumberIdentifier, including cloud environment and raw ID considerations.
The assistant recommends removing token value logging and keeping only expiry time, success status, and correlation identifiers.

best_practices: Use proactive token refresh for clients that remain active beyond one token lifetime.
Log token metadata only, such as expiry time and refresh status, never token values.
Use typed communication identifiers instead of passing raw strings through application code.
anti_patterns: Do not copy access tokens into logs, traces, screenshots, or support tickets.
Do not use static tokens for long-running clients that need continuous access.
Do not treat all raw communication identifiers as the same Java type.

faq.question: What Java package does this skill cover?
faq.answer: It covers com.azure:azure-communication-common for Azure Communication Services authentication and identifiers.

faq.question: Does this skill generate ACS access tokens?
faq.answer: No. It explains client-side credential patterns. Token issuance must happen in your trusted service.

faq.question: Can I use it with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The marketplace metadata marks it as compatible with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Does it support Entra ID authentication?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes an example using EntraCommunicationTokenCredentialOptions for Teams Phone Extensibility scenarios.

faq.question: What is the main security concern?
faq.answer: One example logs part of a token. Avoid logging token values and prefer metadata-only diagnostics.

faq.question: Is this a complete production guide?
faq.answer: No. It is a focused reference for common classes and patterns. Production systems still need full validation and error handling.
